1.pericope_label: Lot and the Angels in Sodom
1.synthesis: Two angels reach Sodom at evening; Lot, sitting in the gate, urges them with Abraham-like hospitality to lodge in his house (vv.1–3). But the men of the city, “both young and old, all the people to the last man,” surround the house demanding to abuse the visitors (vv.4–5). Lot’s attempt to placate them — even offering his daughters (v.8) — fails; they press violently until the angels strike them with blindness and pull Lot inside (vv.10–11). The integrative thread is the exposure of Sodom’s total corruption: the “outcry” is fully justified as the whole city, with no exception, turns to predatory violence against the helpless guest.
1.historical_context: Hospitality to the stranger was sacred in the ancient world, making the city’s assault a grievous inversion of all decency. The demand “that we may know them” (v.5) is the sexual violence Scripture names as part of Sodom’s guilt (Jude 7), though the prophets stress also its pride and cruelty to the poor (Ezekiel 16:49). Lot, “sitting in the gate” (v.1), had risen to civic standing, yet his compromised position is laid bare; his offer of his daughters (v.8) shows how the city’s ethics had eroded even the righteous man (2 Peter 2:7).
1.literary_structure: The scene is a deliberate dark mirror of Genesis 18:1–8: the same gracious reception of divine visitors, now set against a backdrop of mob violence. “To the last man” (v.4) underscores the failure of Abraham’s search for ten righteous. The striking of the mob with blindness (v.11) is poetic judgment — they grope after sin until they cannot find the door — and foreshadows the total blindness of judgment to come.
1.theological_application: Sin, when a culture sanctions it “to the last man,” becomes consuming and self-destructive. Lot’s presence in Sodom warns of the cost of pitching one’s tent toward the city of sin (Genesis 13:12); the righteous who settle among the wicked are vexed and morally compromised (2 Peter 2:7–8). The angels’ rescue assures that God preserves his own even amid pervasive evil, but the episode is a sober call not to grow comfortable in Sodom.
1.christology: The angels who deliver Lot from the mob and the coming fire picture the salvation of the righteous out of judgment (2 Peter 2:9), accomplished fully by Christ, who rescues us “from the wrath to come” (1 Thessalonians 1:10). The blindness that falls on the violent (v.11) anticipates the judicial blinding of those who refuse the light (John 9:39). Lot’s flawed righteousness reminds us that the only secure refuge is the righteousness given in Christ, not our own standing in the gate.

12.synthesis: The angels press Lot to gather his family and flee; his sons-in-law think he jests (v.14). As dawn breaks the angels seize the hands of the lingering Lot, his wife, and daughters, “the LORD being merciful to him,” and set them outside (v.16), commanding, “Escape for your life. Do not look back” (v.17). Lot is permitted to flee to Zoar; then “the LORD rained on Sodom and Gomorrah sulfur and fire from the LORD out of heaven” (v.24). Lot’s wife looks back and becomes a pillar of salt (v.26). The thread is judgment executed and mercy interwoven: God destroys the cities utterly, yet drags the reluctant righteous to safety, “remembering Abraham” (v.29).
12.historical_context: The cities of the plain, near the Dead Sea, are overthrown by “sulfur and fire” (v.24), language that becomes Scripture’s standard image of divine judgment (Luke 17:29; Revelation 21:8). Lot’s wife, “a pillar of salt” (v.26), reflects the salt-encrusted desolation of the region and stands as a permanent warning. That God “remembered Abraham” (v.29) when he sent Lot out reveals that Lot’s rescue was the fruit of Abraham’s intercession of the previous chapter.
12.literary_structure: The pace quickens with urgent imperatives — “Up! Get out… Escape… do not look back” (v.14, v.17) — building tension against Lot’s lingering. The note that God “remembered Abraham” (v.29) is the theological key, framing the whole destruction as the answer to the intercession of Genesis 18:23–32. Lot’s wife’s backward look (v.26) is the narrative’s sharp moral pivot, a single act epitomizing a divided heart.
12.theological_application: “Remember Lot’s wife” (Luke 17:32): Jesus himself makes her the emblem of those who, called to flee, cling in heart to what is doomed. The passage teaches the certainty of judgment on entrenched wickedness and, equally, the mercy that delivers the undeserving for the sake of another’s prayers (v.29). It warns against half-hearted obedience — lingering, looking back, bargaining for Zoar — and calls for decisive flight from sin toward the refuge God provides (Hebrews 6:18). The episode also exposes the peril of half-hearted obedience: Lot lingers, bargains for Zoar, and his wife looks back, so that even the rescued bear the marks of the world they were slow to leave. Believers are summoned to decisive flight from sin and to fix their hope on the refuge God provides, not on the doomed comforts behind them (Philippians 3:13–14).
12.christology: The deliverance of Lot “because God remembered Abraham” (v.29) displays the principle of salvation through another’s mediation, fulfilled in Christ, for whose sake the Father remembers and spares his people. Sodom’s fire prefigures final judgment (2 Peter 3:7), from which Christ alone delivers. His call “do not turn back” (Luke 9:62) echoes the angel’s command, summoning whole-hearted discipleship that does not look longingly at the city under wrath.

30.synthesis: Afraid to remain in Zoar, Lot dwells in a cave with his two daughters (v.30). Believing themselves without prospect of marriage, the daughters make their father drunk on successive nights and lie with him, each conceiving (vv.31–35). Their sons are Moab and Ben-ammi, ancestors of the Moabites and Ammonites (vv.37–38). The integrative thread is the squalid aftermath of Sodom: the man rescued from the city carries its moral ruin into the cave, and his line is perpetuated through incest — a sober coda showing how deeply the culture of Sodom had corrupted even the saved.
30.historical_context: The episode explains the origin of two of Israel’s perennial neighbors and adversaries, the Moabites and Ammonites (Deuteronomy 23:3). The daughters, shaped by Sodom’s ethics, act on the fear that “there is not a man on earth” (v.31), resorting to deception and incest rather than trusting providence. Commentators (Calvin, Henry) read the account as neither approving nor merely salacious, but as a candid record of sin’s legacy and a warning against the company of the wicked.
30.literary_structure: The narrative’s flat, unflinching reportage refuses to gloss the shame, much as it earlier refused to gloss Sodom’s. The naming of Moab and Ben-ammi (vv.37–38) closes the Lot cycle on a note of ironic fruitfulness — life continues, but tainted. Lot, last seen drunk and deceived, simply disappears from the narrative, his decline complete, a foil to Abraham who walks on in covenant blessing.
30.theological_application: The passage exposes the long shadow sin casts: rescued from Sodom, Lot’s family still bears Sodom within. It warns parents and households that the moral environment we choose shapes our children more than we know (1 Corinthians 15:33). Yet even here grace is not wholly absent — for from Moab will come Ruth, and from Ruth, David and ultimately Christ (Ruth 4:17), so that God weaves redemption even through a shameful origin. The passage refuses sentimentality about the rescued: deliverance from judgment is not the same as deliverance from sin’s inward grip, and Lot’s family proves it. Yet the line is not abandoned by God, for from Moab he will in time draw Ruth into the lineage of the Messiah (Ruth 1:4), a quiet testimony that grace can redeem even a shameful origin and that no one is beyond the reach of his mercy.
30.christology: That Moab, born of this disgrace, becomes the lineage of Ruth and so of the Messiah (Matthew 1:5) magnifies the grace of God, who gathers the unlikely and the unclean into the line of his Son. As Tamar, Rahab, and Ruth in Christ’s genealogy attest, the gospel redeems scandalous beginnings, and the Savior is not ashamed to descend through them (Hebrews 2:11), bringing outsiders like Moab into the family of promise.
